PHASE: Phase 7 active — eighth Feature Update merged. Socket.IO realtime foundation now live: instrumentation.ts hook bootstraps a Socket.IO HTTP server on SOCKET_PORT (43515 dev = base+13, separate from APP_PORT). io.use(socketAuthMiddleware) verifies the Auth.js v5 session cookie on every handshake using next-auth/jwt decode with the cookie name as JWE salt; rejects unauthenticated handshakes; populates socket.data.session with the same narrowed shape as session.user (organizationSlug, organizationId, role, isSuperAdmin, securityVersion). Org-scoped channels use the locked `${organizationId}:${eventType}` naming (DECISIONS_LOG line 173); `joinOrgChannel(socket, eventType)` takes NO orgId parameter so cross-org subscription via API surface is impossible. Super-admin parallel `platform:${eventType}` channels reachable only via `joinPlatformChannel` (checks session.isSuperAdmin). 60s session-revalidation loop walks io.fetchSockets(), dedupes userIds, single platformPrisma.user.findMany including organization.suspended_at; emits "session:invalidated" + disconnects sockets failing any of: missing user / status != active / org suspended / security_version mismatch. Closes Phase 7 #8 candidate (e) — was the highest-impact open ticket per .whatsnext.

LESSONS_ADDED (3 new entries):
  🟤 Auth.js v5 JWE cookie-name-as-salt: decode() needs the SAME cookie name that wrapped the token as `salt` parameter. Passing the wrong salt returns null even with the right secret. Cookie name differs by env: authjs.session-token (dev) / __Secure-authjs.session-token (prod).
  🟤 Cross-org subscription guard via API-surface design: `joinOrgChannel(socket, eventType)` takes NO `organizationId` parameter — it always reads `socket.data.session.organizationId`. The absence of an org-id input IS the protection; no runtime check needed because there is no surface to attack.
  🟤 Socket.IO hosting topology = option B (instrumentation.ts + separate port). Locked in DECISIONS_LOG (line ~175+). Alternatives (A custom server) and (C separate package) deferred. The current pattern can be extracted to (C) by moving the factory + revalidation + tests to a new package with zero changes to auth logic.

TIER_CLASSIFICATION: 3 — heavy. 8 new files + 4 modified across 2 modules (instrumentation + server/socket/*). Score (files=12 × 2.5) + (modules=2 × 5) + (dependency_depth=3 × 3) = 49, above the 40 threshold. Decomposed into 3 sub-sessions: e-1 (~30K context), e-2 (~25K), e-3 (~15K governance). All Opus self-executed per Phase 7 #7's [[sonnet-thrash-sessionstart-hooks]] precedent.

PHASE_7_TICKET_8_NOTES:
  - Why separate port (option B) over custom server (option A): Next.js's `output: 'standalone'` Docker build pattern + `next start` workflow assumes the default HTTP server. A custom server.ts replaces both, requiring a custom Dockerfile and breaking the standalone bundle. Option B keeps Next.js's deployment story intact and adds Socket.IO as a sidecar process on a different port. Cost: two ports to manage (APP_PORT + SOCKET_PORT) and CORS configuration for the cross-origin cookie flow. Benefit: zero changes to next.config.ts, Dockerfile, or `next start` semantics.
  - Why instrumentation.ts (not server.ts) for the bootstrap: Next.js 15 added `instrumentation.ts` specifically as the place to run code at server start. Loaded by Next.js itself; gated by NEXT_RUNTIME=nodejs (Edge runtime also evaluates this file but Socket.IO is Node-only). Dynamic imports of http, socket.io, and @/server/socket/* keep this file Edge-bundle-safe so it doesn't break the middleware's Edge runtime.
  - Why cookie-name salting matters: Auth.js v5 introduced a domain-separation feature where each cookie's JWE is salted with the cookie name. This prevents a token harvested from one Auth.js cookie (e.g., __Secure-authjs.session-token) from being usable as another (e.g., authjs.session-token in a dev tunnel). The Socket auth middleware reads the cookie name based on env.NODE_ENV and passes it as the `salt` argument to next-auth/jwt decode. Got this right the first time because Phase 7 #7c-1's session callback flow made the structure clear.
  - Why cross-org guard at API-surface (not runtime check): the natural Socket.IO pattern is `socket.join(roomName)`. If `roomName` is a user-controlled input, runtime authorization is needed. But if `roomName` is computed from `session.organizationId` inside a helper, there's no input to authorize — the org id is server-controlled. `joinOrgChannel(socket, eventType)` deliberately has no org-id parameter so the only way to reach `socket.join(`${otherOrg}:${event}`)` is to write fresh code that bypasses the helper. This pattern is the same as the Prisma `$allOperations` tenant-guard extension (L6): correct-by-construction beats authorize-at-runtime.
  - Why revalidate inside the loop (not per-message): a malicious user whose session has been invalidated server-side (role demoted, suspended, password reset) could continue receiving messages until the next handshake. The 60s loop is the upper bound on staleness — acceptable for non-financial real-time use cases (presence, chat, ringing). For high-stakes events (e.g., super-admin tenant-disable), the platform code can also call `io.fetchSockets()` directly and disconnect targeted sockets out-of-band (not implemented here; would be added with the first feature that needs it).
